content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 5 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 5 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 5:1 -- "sexual immorality / not tolerated / not found" and "has his father's wife / living in sin with his stepmother"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It is actually heard [that there is] sexual immorality among you, and such sexual immorality as is not [found] even among the nations -- that someone has the wife of the father" | akouetai as "heard"; ethnesin as "nations"; "wife of the father" literal |
| LOGOS | "It is actually reported that there is sexual immorality among you, and of a kind that is not tolerated even among pagans, for a man has his father's wife" | akouetai as "reported"; ethnesin as "pagans"; "father's wife" |
| PNEUMA | "I can hardly believe the report about the sexual immorality going on among you -- something that even pagans don't do. I am told that a man in your church is living in sin with his stepmother" | Adds "I can hardly believe"; specifies "stepmother"; "living in sin with" interprets echo |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Reported" for akouetai is standard. "Father's wife" preserves the Levitical phrasing (Lev 18:8) without PNEUMA's specific "stepmother" (which is likely correct but interpretive). PNEUMA's "I can hardly believe" and "living in sin with" add emotional and moral commentary beyond the Greek. EMET's "nations" vs. LOGOS's "pagans" -- "pagans" better communicates the point to English readers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:2 -- "arrogant / puffed up / so proud of yourselves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And you are puffed up! And did you not rather mourn, that the [one] having done this deed might be removed from your midst?" | physiomai as "puffed up" (Paul's key term) |
| LOGOS | "And you are arrogant! Ought you not rather to mourn? Let him who has done this be removed from among you" | physiomai as "arrogant" |
| PNEUMA | "You are so proud of yourselves, but you should be mourning in sorrow and shame. And you should remove this man from your fellowship" | physiomai as "proud of yourselves"; adds "in sorrow and shame"; "fellowship" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Arrogant" captures the sense of physiomai while being more natural than "puffed up" (EMET, though literally closer). PNEUMA's "so proud of yourselves" adds intensifiers and "in sorrow and shame" expands pentheo ("to mourn"). PNEUMA's "remove this man from your fellowship" shifts agency -- the Greek passive suggests God's or the community's action, not just their decision.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:3-4 -- Complex sentence structure and "spirit" reference

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For I indeed, being absent in the body but present in the spirit, have already judged...in the name of the Lord Yeshua, you being gathered together and the spirit of me, with the power of the Lord of us Yeshua" | Preserves complex participial chain |
| LOGOS | "For though absent in body, I am present in spirit; and as if present, I have already pronounced judgment...When you are assembled in the name of the Lord Jesus, and my spirit is present, with the power of our Lord Jesus" | Breaks into clearer English clauses |
| PNEUMA | "Even though I am not with you in person, I am with you in the Spirit. And as though I were there, I have already passed judgment...in the name of the Lord Jesus. You must call a meeting of the church. I will be present with you in spirit, and so will the power of our Lord Jesus" | Capitalizes "Spirit"; adds "You must call a meeting of the church" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The complex Greek syntax of vv.3-5 (one long sentence) must be broken into clauses for English. LOGOS does this most effectively. PNEUMA's "You must call a meeting of the church" is an interpretive insertion. The capitalization of "Spirit" (PNEUMA) is debatable -- Paul may mean his own human spirit present among them, not the Holy Spirit specifically.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Spirit/spirit capitalization noted as interpretive.

---

## 5:5 -- "deliver to Satan / hand over to Satan / throw out and hand over"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to deliver such a [one] to the Satan for [the] destruction of the flesh, that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord" | paradidomi as "deliver"; includes article "the Satan" |
| LOGOS | "you are to deliver this man to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord" | Same verb; adds "his" before spirit |
| PNEUMA | "Then you must throw this man out and hand him over to Satan so that his sinful nature will be destroyed and he himself will be saved on the day the Lord returns" | "throw this man out and hand him over"; sarx as "sinful nature"; adds "the Lord returns" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Deliver to Satan" is the standard translation of paradidomi to Satana. PNEUMA's "throw this man out and hand him over" splits one action into two. More significantly, PNEUMA renders "destruction of the flesh" as "sinful nature will be destroyed" -- this is a major interpretive choice. The Greek sarx ("flesh") here likely means the physical body or the fleshly mode of existence, not the Pauline "sinful nature" (which is a specific interpretation of sarx). PNEUMA also adds "the Lord returns" to "the day of the Lord."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "sinful nature" for sarx flagged as over-interpretation.

---

## 5:7 -- "Passover lamb / Passover" and "cleanse out / get rid of"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Cleanse out the old leaven...For indeed Christos, the Passover of us, was sacrificed" | to pascha as "Passover" (without "lamb") |
| LOGOS | "Cleanse out the old leaven...For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed" | Adds "lamb" to pascha |
| PNEUMA | "Get rid of the old 'yeast' by removing this wicked person from among you...Christ, our Passover Lamb, has been sacrificed for us" | "Get rid of" for ekkathairein; adds "by removing this wicked person"; adds "for us"; "yeast" in quotes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Adding "lamb" to "Passover" is a legitimate clarification -- pascha in this context refers to the sacrificial lamb (the verb ethythe, "was sacrificed," confirms this). EMET's bare "Passover" is ambiguous in English (feast or lamb?). PNEUMA's "by removing this wicked person" is an interpretive insertion connecting the metaphor to the specific case. "For us" is not in the Greek at this point (though theologically implied).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:8 -- "celebrate the festival / celebrate the feast"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "let us celebrate the feast, not with old leaven, nor with [the] leaven of malice and evil, but with [the] unleavened [bread] of sincerity and truth" | heortazo as "celebrate the feast" |
| LOGOS | "Let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" | "festival" for heorte |
| PNEUMA | "So let us celebrate the festival, not with the old bread of wickedness and evil, but with the new bread of sincerity and truth" | Changes "leaven" to "bread" in first clause; adds "new" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA changes "the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil" to "the old bread of wickedness and evil," which conflates the leaven metaphor with the bread. Paul's point is that the leaven (a permeating agent) represents malice and evil -- it is what contaminates the bread, not the bread itself. Adding "new" before the unleavened bread is not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's conflation of leaven and bread noted as an error.

---

## 5:9 -- "my letter / the letter / before"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I wrote to you in the letter not to associate with sexually immoral [persons]" | en te epistole as "in the letter" (with article, indicating a specific letter) |
| LOGOS | "I wrote to you in my letter not to associate with sexually immoral people" | "my letter" |
| PNEUMA | "When I wrote to you before, I told you not to associate with people who indulge in sexual sin" | "before" replaces letter reference; "told" for grapho; "indulge in sexual sin" expands |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "In my letter" identifies the previous (now lost) correspondence. PNEUMA's "before" loses the specific reference to a written document. "Indulge in sexual sin" is more expansive than the simple pornois ("sexually immoral persons").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:11 -- "bears the name of brother / called a brother / claims to be a believer" and "reviler / abusive"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not to associate with [anyone] called a brother who is a sexually immoral person...or a reviler, or a drunkard" | onomazomenos as "called"; loidoros as "reviler" |
| LOGOS | "not to associate with anyone who bears the name of brother if he is guilty of sexual immorality...reviler, drunkard" | "bears the name of" |
| PNEUMA | "you are not to associate with anyone who claims to be a believer yet indulges in sexual sin...or is abusive" | "claims to be a believer" for adelphos; "abusive" for loidoros |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Bears the name of brother" preserves the Greek idiom (onomazomenos adelphos) and the familial language of adelphos. PNEUMA's "claims to be a believer" modernizes but loses "brother" (the church as family). "Reviler" (LOGOS/EMET) for loidoros is more precise than "abusive" (PNEUMA), as it specifically means verbal abuse/slander.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:13 -- "Purge/Remove the evil person/one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Remove the evil [one] from among yourselves" | exairo as "remove"; poneros as "evil one" |
| LOGOS | "Purge the evil person from among you" | exairo as "purge" |
| PNEUMA | "You must remove the evil person from among you" | Adds "You must" (imperative emphasis) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Purge" echoes the Deuteronomic formula (Deut 17:7 LXX) from which Paul quotes. EMET's "remove" is accurate but less resonant with the OT source. PNEUMA's "You must" adds emphasis not in the citation form.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interprets sarx as "sinful nature" | v.5 | "sinful nature will be destroyed" vs. "destruction of the flesh" |
| PNEUMA adds emotional/editorial commentary | vv.1, 2 | "I can hardly believe," "so proud of yourselves" |
| PNEUMA inserts interpretive connections | vv.3-4, 7 | "You must call a meeting"; "by removing this wicked person" |
| PNEUMA conflates or expands metaphors | v.8 | Leaven/bread conflation |
| EMET preserves Greek article and naming patterns | vv.1, 5 | "the nations," "the Satan" |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment | Throughout | LOGOS as base text |
| OT citation fidelity | v.13 | "Purge" (LOGOS) vs. "Remove" (EMET) |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 5 presents a case of severe church discipline, and the translator divergences are most significant where the language is most charged. PNEUMA's tendency to interpret sarx as "sinful nature" (v.5) is a recurring concern that affects Pauline anthropology. PNEUMA's conflation of leaven and bread in v.8 is a genuine translation error that obscures Paul's metaphor. The chapter also shows PNEUMA inserting explanatory connectors ("by removing this wicked person" in v.7) that collapse the distance between Paul's metaphorical and practical language -- Paul uses the Passover metaphor to make a theological argument, and PNEUMA prematurely resolves it into practical instruction. LOGOS maintains the distinction between metaphor and application.

---
